# UC-10.8.35 · Container Runtime Security Event Correlation

## Description

Container escape or privileged container abuse can lead to host compromise. Correlating runtime events with image and orchestrator data improves detection of container-based attacks.

## Value

Container escape or privileged container abuse can lead to host compromise. Correlating runtime events with image and orchestrator data improves detection of container-based attacks.

## Implementation

Ingest container runtime and K8s audit logs. Alert on privileged pod creation, exec into production namespaces, or high-severity runtime events. Correlate with image provenance and network flows.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Container security TAs, Kubernetes audit logs.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Container runtime events, K8s audit log, image registry.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Ingest container runtime and K8s audit logs. Alert on privileged pod creation, exec into production namespaces, or high-severity runtime events. Correlate with image provenance and network flows.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=containers (sourcetype="kube:audit" OR sourcetype="container:runtime")
| search (reason="PrivilegedContainers" OR action="exec" OR severity="high")
| stats count by pod, namespace, user, action
| where count > 10
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Container Runtime Security Event Correlation** — Container escape or privileged container abuse can lead to host compromise. Correlating runtime events with image and orchestrator data improves detection of container-based attacks.

Documented **Data sources**: Container runtime events, K8s audit log, image registry. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Container security TAs, Kubernetes audit logs. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: containers; **sourcetype**: kube:audit, container:runtime. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=containers, sourcetype="kube:audit". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by pod, namespace, user, action** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where count > 10` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with certificate authority records, an inventory of endpoints, or `openssl x509 -text` on a sample file so expiry and subject data in Splunk match the source of truth.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (security events by pod), Timeline (runtime events), Bar chart (events by namespace).

## SPL

```spl
index=containers (sourcetype="kube:audit" OR sourcetype="container:runtime")
| search (reason="PrivilegedContainers" OR action="exec" OR severity="high")
| stats count by pod, namespace, user, action
| where count > 10
| sort -count
```

## Visualization

Table (security events by pod), Timeline (runtime events), Bar chart (events by namespace).
